definition: Action-Centered Academic Design — a framework for constructing rigorous, systems-thinking arguments that trace from root cause to measurable impact.
A visual strategic tool used in ACAD to map a complete system design, from problem identification to impact measurement, across multiple stakeholder layers.
The definitive reason why one system design outperforms another in a specific context, accounting for feasibility, scalability, and impact magnitude.
The traceable sequence linking specific activities to measurable long-term changes — the argumentative spine of any ACAD case.
The ability of a system to grow in scope or be replicated faithfully across different geographies, populations, or institutional contexts.
The operational reality and practical likelihood of a system working in the real world, accounting for political, financial, and logistical constraints.
The underlying structural issue that, if left unaddressed, perpetuates the harm regardless of surface-level interventions.
The structural barrier — built into status-quo policy, law, or practice — that prevents the problem from being solved without the proposed change.
The mechanism by which the proposed plan eliminates the identified harm — the bridge between your action and your impact.
The size and severity of a harm or benefit argument, measured in scope (how many affected), intensity (how severely), and reversibility.
The likelihood that a harm will occur or that a solvency mechanism will function as described, supported by empirical or historical evidence.
The urgency dimension of an impact argument — how quickly the harm materializes or how rapidly the benefit can be realized.
Whether a harm, once occurred, can be undone — irreversible harms (e.g., extinction, structural discrimination) are typically prioritized in impact calculus.
The step-by-step logical sequence from a policy action to its downstream effects, each link requiring independent evidential support.
An analysis of what would happen in the absence of the proposed plan — establishes the baseline against which the plan's benefits are measured.
The present state of the world — policies, practices, and conditions as they currently exist — used as the baseline for impact comparisons.
The argument that the impact being described does not already exist in the status quo and is exclusively caused by the plan or the opposing position.
The argumentative connection between a plan action and a downstream disadvantage or benefit — the first step in any impact chain.
A negative consequence uniquely triggered by the opposing plan, structured as: Uniqueness → Link → Internal Link → Impact.
The planned approach for communicating a policy position to decision-makers, stakeholders, and the public to generate support and drive action.
A comprehensive map of how and why a set of activities will produce the desired outcome — articulates assumptions at each stage of the causal chain.
Identification and assessment of all parties affected by or capable of affecting a proposed system — essential for feasibility and political viability.
The strategic act of defining the scope, cause, and population of a crisis in a way that positions your solution as necessary and sufficient.
Intervention at the structural level — altering the rules, institutions, or norms that produce outcomes — rather than addressing symptoms alone.
A specific mechanism through which a governing or institutional authority can effect change — e.g., legislation, regulation, funding allocation, or executive order.
The corpus of empirical data, expert testimony, historical precedent, and peer-reviewed research that supports every major claim in an ACAD case.
The structured presentation of all negative consequences of the status quo that the proposed plan is designed to resolve.
The precise, one-paragraph statement of what the advocating team proposes to do — must be specific, enforceable, and agent-clear.
A phase-by-phase schedule for enacting the proposed system, demonstrating operational realism and managing resource deployment.
The system of metrics, evaluation checkpoints, and feedback loops used to assess whether a policy is achieving its intended outcomes.
The overall assessment that the advantages of a plan outweigh its disadvantages — the core calculus in impact-driven debate.
A test of competition that argues the affirmative plan and the negative counterplan can be implemented together, negating the either/or choice.
A negative team's alternative proposal that competes with the affirmative plan by being mutually exclusive and offering superior net benefits.
A procedural argument that the affirmative plan falls outside the intended scope of the resolution — a jurisdictional check on the debate.
A philosophical or structural challenge to the assumptions, framework, or language of the opposing case — operates at the level of epistemology or ethics.
An ACAD content design principle: each concept is self-contained in its own visual card module, allowing non-linear navigation and deep focus.
The ACAD design philosophy: premium typography, high-contrast aesthetics, and editorial restraint produce an environment of intellectual seriousness.
An app-like scrolling engine that pre-loads curriculum modules for a seamless, infinite-scroll flow between chapters.
The obligation on the advancing party to substantiate their claims with sufficient evidence — in debate, the affirmative typically carries the initial burden.
The conventional assumption in policy debate that the judge can hypothetically enact the proposed plan, bypassing questions of political likelihood.
The process of weighing competing impacts against each other using shared criteria such as magnitude, probability, timeframe, and reversibility.
A structured questioning period designed to expose logical weaknesses, missing evidence, definitional ambiguity, or strategic inconsistency in an opponent's case.
A structured note-taking system used in competitive debate to track each argument's evolution across all speeches — the record of the round.
